data.title: Dell's AI Payday Just Got Way Bigger - By 2x
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The market for AI servers is one of the toughest areas to compete in within the AI infrastructure ecosystem.

Competition is fierce, inventory management is a nightmare, and margins are extremely tight, leaving very little room for error. In my opinion, these kinds of setups don't always tend to offer investors alpha-generating opportunities.

However, Dell Technologies Inc. (NYSE:DELL) is one of those companies that stands out for lots of reasons. The Texas-based global technology giant has forged deep partnerships on both ends of the spectrum, with vendors like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and hypergrowth customers like CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), raising its AI prospects. Its PC and storage business is set for inflection with those end markets on the mend. And the company is seeing such robust growth in its AI server business that it 2x-ed its long-term growth forecasts at the recently held 2025 Analyst Day.

The significant increase in Dell’s forward-looking long-term estimates denotes the strong AI momentum the company is expecting to benefit from as demand for its AI servers keeps surging.

I am reiterating my Buy rating and raising my PT on Dell.

AI Is Fast-Forwarding Dell’s Growth Prospects

I have written about Dell a few times already, and I advised investors to ignore the extreme focus that markets have on the company’s margin profile. Like I mentioned earlier, margin expansion is extremely important for the markets when it comes to expectations from Dell and its peers in the AI server market. This is because most of the players in this space, including Dell, tend to operate their business on mid-single operating margins, which are razor-thin.

Given the vast opportunities that demand for AI infrastructure provides for raising the operating leverage, generally speaking,

Markets tend to think that the elevated demand for AI infrastructure buildouts should lead to stronger operating leverage, which generally is the case, except for the AI server end market.

General expectations in the market were for Dell’s ISG segment (Infrastructure Solutions Group) to guide/report full-year FY26 operating margins of ~11.5%. The deceleration in Dell ISG’s operating margins to 8.8% in Q2 FY26 complicated that path to ~11.5% operating margins, and markets seemed not to like that one bit, with Dell’s shares selling off after the Q2 report.

The gross margin deceleration to 18% from the usual ~22% quarterly gross margin levels rubbed more salt into the wounds of Dell’s shares. Markets ignored the accelerating 19.6% y/y growth in Q2 consolidated revenues and the 44% jump in Q2 ISG revenues. (That’s how focused markets are on Dell and other server players' margin expansion).

However, Dell surprised investors with its 2025 Analyst Day, from Tuesday this week, where management made some strong projections about the growth runways that lie ahead for the company, especially for its AI servers business and the possible margin accretion that investors can expect ahead.

Let’s start with Dell’s ISG business outlook and navigate to their consolidated outlook that will eventually move the needle on its valuation.

Management believes Dell ISG’s revenues will swell to $55.3B in FY26 this year, a 27% jump from the previous year. Investors can already see in the chart below how revenues generated from shipping AI servers, like its PowerEdge AI servers, have propelled Dell’s ISG revenue growth trajectory beyond the 8% CAGR.

The boost that Dell got from selling AI servers is significant. Management revealed they expect to generate $20B from selling their PowerEdge AI servers this year, implying a staggering 253% CAGR in the last two years. Dell is outselling the market for AI servers, which is expected to grow 45% this year, according to IDC’s latest figures.

Dell also indicated, in the 2025 Analyst Day meeting, that margins for its ISG group will land between 10-14%, higher than Wall Street’s expectations of 11.5%. I noted previously. There are two key growth levers Dell is leaning on to raise the operating leverage.

First is Dell IP—the company’s homegrown solutions for storage & proprietary server designs that can help Dell capture more value and avoid reliance on third-party vendors. This will be a key differentiator for Dell, allowing it to work more closely with its deep-pocketed customers and helping it compete with fast-growing Asian server makers like Foxconn, Wistron, etc.

Second is that they see growth in the enterprise segment of their customer base. At the moment, their AI server sales are heavily skewed towards hyperscalers and frontier LLM makers like OpenAI and xAI. But doing business with such an innovative roster of clients is raising the credibility profile of Dell among the enterprise market. Dell revealed that it sold AI servers to more than 3000 unique customers last year and has a line of sight to sell to 6700 enterprise customers.

What Dell’s AI business has done for the company is that it has doubled the expectations that management now has, from growing towards a long-term target range of 3-4% to now growing towards a 7-9% long-term target range. Also, as seen below, even management’s EPS long-term growth expectations have doubled to 15%.

Investors can also go to Seeking Alpha’s Earnings Revisions tool and see how markets had to meaningfully amp up their expectations on Dell because they were behind (or pessimistic) in their outlook.

Markets were expecting Dell to grow consolidated FY27 revenues by ~7% to $115.2B, but the new long-term expectations Dell set pushed markets to rethink their own long-term expectations. Consensus estimates for FY27 revenue expectations have now been raised to $117.2B, up ~9% y/y, while the market’s FY26 revenue expectations have now settled at $107.7B, up 12.7% y/y.

My PT for Dell Indicates Upside Next Year

In my strong opinion, the case for being bullish on Dell should not be ignored.

Keeping management’s new outlook in mind, I concur with the market's current expectations of Dell growing its revenues 12.7% in FY26 and 9% in FY27. Dell is also expected to grow its EPS by ~17.2% per year in FY26 and FY27. For such growth rates, I believe a forward PE of 18-19x is warranted because that is what markets have typically valued the company at.

Currently, Dell is trading at 17.2x FY26 EPS, and it could be reaching the upper end of its fair value range for now. Therefore, comparing 17.2 vs. 18-19x implies about 4.7-9% further upside in Dell’s shares.

However, if investors are considering the next year, which is what markets are doing as we enter the final quarter of 2025, Dell looks attractive. As seen above, Dell’s shares trade at 14.7x FY27 EPS, which is also expected to grow ~17.2%, mirroring FY26’s EPS growth expectations. This implies a ~22-25% upside in Dell’s shares over the course of FY27 or CY26 or a PT of ~$197 per share.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

My bullish thesis on Dell is based on my analysis of Dell’s forward-looking long-term projections. This is also dependent on Dell actually hitting their margin projections of 10-14% as stated. However, the market can get tougher, as I noted at the start of this post, which could impede Dell from raising its margin profile, especially in the ISG business that accounts for AI server shipments.

Additionally, Dell’s PC business has finally started reporting some growth after years of sales contractions. If PC sales reverse to the downside, expect pressure on the overall sales and margins.

Takeaway

Dell’s efforts in capturing demand in the AI server market have elevated the growth outlook for the company and pushed it out of the meager low single-digit growth rates investors were used to seeing pre-ChatGPT to high single-digit growth rates investors can now expect.

Versus management’s newly minted growth outlook, Dell’s shares look very attractive. I have raised my PT on Dell, and I reiterate my Buy rating.

data.title: Joby Aviation: Shares Fall After Capital Raise, A Necessary Step Toward eVTOL Commercialization
data.description:
Joby Aviation, Inc. (NYSE:JOBY) stock tumbled 9% following a $514 million capital raise. The stock is now down 5.5% since my last report in which I marked Joby Aviation as a Buy. In my view, capital raises such as the one announced are part of the game for developers of eVTOL in an effort to transform urban mobility. In this report, I discuss the capital raise and attach a price target to Joby Aviation stock.

Capital Raise In Support Of eVTOL Certification And Manufacturing

While share prices have fallen since the announcement of the capital raise, I believe this is the ordinary course of business for eVTOL developers. Opportunities for urban air mobility are huge, but the reality is that developing an eVTOL is capital-intensive. For many companies that aim to capitalize on a transformative technology or development, it currently seems that the sky is the limit. That, however, undermines the reality that for many companies there is no revenue, let alone cash flow, to fund the development, and accessing the debt markets to raise funds is often also not an option. That leaves equity raises as the only realistic path to fund development. It is also not the case that with having an eVTOL built or developed that the capitalization runway starts. Before that, the certification path is part of the development and the initial production of the early models is likely going to be loss-making. So, capital raises right now are the only solution and that means as a shareholder you have to accept that risk or time your position well to minimize dilution risk.

Joby Aviation will be offering 30.5 million shares priced at $16.85 with gross proceeds of $513.9 million. On top of that there are options to raise an additional $77.1 million by offering an additional 4.575 million shares. The offering happened at an 11% discount, which I believe is actually not a bad discount at all. Proceeds will be used to fund certification and manufacturing. So, it is really the playbook capital raise I would say.

The Impact On Joby Aviation Stock Price Targets

Modeling the dilution to the share price is actually not very straightforward. Ideally, I use an EV/EBITDA valuation method. That is already where we run into some issues as the company is expected to produce EBITDA losses of around $500 million annually through 2027. So, better-suited methods to model everything are through EV/Sales. However, that also comes with a bit of a drawback as the company is not expected to ramp up sales until 2027. Therefore, whatever sales projection we end up with for 2027, it is just the start of the revenue ramp.

If we apply a price-to-book valuation at 16x P/B and assume that the optional 4.575 million shares will also be issued, we end up with around 12% upside taking the lowest upside figure between 2025 and 2027. Absent revenues and EBITDA, the P/B also appears inflated. We note that Joby Aviation traded around 5-6 times P/B, which is now substantially higher. So, the price-to-book is not an extremely good indicator either. In fact, if we put the price-to-book at two times the median for Joby, we get to 16-20 percent downside. Perhaps that downside provides us with a bit of an indication of when Joby Aviation stock becomes a strong buy.

At 6x EV/book value, which is roughly where the company used to trade, there is 45-50 percent downside, and at 12x EV/book value, we note that Joby Aviation is more or less fairly valued. So, the capital raise has not opened a major opportunity from a value perspective. To me, it is an ordinary capital raise that fits in the company’s commercialization path and I do expect that capital raises will be needed for the next few years.

Conclusion: Capital Raise Was Nothing Exceptional For Joby Aviation

The stock price did tank on the news of the capital raise, but I consider it to be the most logical path at this point to raise funds as eVTOL developers prepare their businesses for certification of their products and production ramp-up. With that ramp-up in mind and the associated capital requirements, I do believe additional capital raises will be required and at 12x P/B, the stock is more or less fairly valued for this year and I keep my speculative buy rating. The impact of future capital raises does depend on the prevailing market prices for the common stock and the discount offered on additional offerings, but to me, the $514 million capital raise was one that followed the trajectory of the company.

data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.: Fairly Valued Global Insurance Broker
data.description:
Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (NYSE:AJG) is a global specialty insurance broker. With uncertainty about tariffs, interest rates, inflation, and many other macroeconomic stressors, many investors look to insurance, and brokers provide the profits without the underwriting risks.

While AJG has been an all-weather grower and has more growth ahead, it's possible to overpay for this, and so it's not quite a Buy today.

Business Model

AJG does not purely engage in brokerage. The two revenue segments consist of:

  • Brokerage: 86% of 2024 Revenue
  • Risk Management: 14% of 2024 Revenue

As the table above from the latest Form 10K shows, brokerage revenues are not necessarily all commissions from the policies they directly sell. Almost a third of revenue is fees they garner not just from their risk management services (where they provide data and analytics to underwriters) but from fees on customer service for underwriters, as well as a retail platform used by other brokers (giving AJG wholesale revenue).

The table above illustrates only about half of brokerage revenues comes from their own direct commissions. It also shows that about 38% of this revenue is from international markets, which are primarily the UK, Australia, Canada, and New Zealand.

Across these geographies, they sell dozens of lines of P&C insurance.

The third segment, which doesn't produce revenue but is an important part of their model, is corporate. I wouldn't normally stress such a segment, but AJG regularly engages in M&A, and this is a major focus of corporate on a regular basis. CEO J. Patrick Gallagher explained in the Q2 earnings call:

Our machine, and we're in just a great position is driven by literally hundreds of people in the field around the world, talking to folks that they admire and working with people who have been hired by folks to sell their business. And we're on that short list of virtually anybody that wants to take a look at possibly selling their enterprise, large or small. Our tuck-in acquisition business purchases are not all $50 million, $100 million. There's lots of 2s, 5s, 10s. These are family businesses, and they're not PE roll-ups and they're looking for a home for their people.

As a brokerage firm is more effective at scale, M&A is an important way to win and keep business, while increasing margins over time.

Financial History

How has such a model treated the company over time? Well, as one might expect of M&A, it's contributed steady growth.

Between 2015 and 2024, annual revenue more than doubled, from $5.3B to $10.9B. This is great for any business, but much how much money finds its way to shareholders?

Above, I've displayed the impact to free cash flow, as well as adjusted FCF that factors in M&A activity, which is visible in every year of the past decade with a lower AFCF figure. Some years even show a net outflow.

Some of this gap has been plugged by issuance of debt. Q2 results showed a long-term debt balance of $12B. While this seems covered by $14.3B in cash, this is not quite the whole case. As they disclosed in their annual and quarterly reports, $8.5B in cash was raised in December 2024 and another $1.28B in January were raised for the acquisition of AssuredPartners, which occurred in August.

As far as what actually gets back to shareholders, most of this is done through dividends, with very limited buybacks in their history. They have steadily increased cash dividends each year, even in years with outflows.

The year 2025 has shown continued growth. Revenue for Q2 was up 16% YoY, across the entire business.

On the cash flow side, half-year results reflect how timing of M&A can impact things. Full-year results are more indicative of AJG's ability to generate cash.

Overall, it's a steady business, one with consistent growth over many years. It's not terribly risky, but one has to question how much growth, organic and through M&A, is still on the table.

Outlook and Valuation

What makes AJG tempting is that it is a solid business, but with a $79B market cap, it is quite large for an insurance broker, and I think we must temper our assumptions about growth. First, let's look at the business metrics with AssuredPartners acquired, per their company update.

The acquisition adds $3B of TTM revenue to AJG's own $12B of TTM revenue. FCF is recently about 20% of total revenue, suggesting that annual FCF going forward should be about $3B. From there it's a matter of how much we want to anticipate more outflows for M&A.

In the post-acquisition conference call, Gallagher noted the following about the pipeline:

Our global pipeline of opportunities with term sheets signed delivered or being prepared includes nearly 40 potential mergers with about $500 million of annualized revenue. So the pipeline remains strong and full of tuck-in merger and acquisition opportunities around the globe that can contribute to our long-term strategic growth.

He went on to add this market data:

The Swiss Re Institute estimates there is more than $7 trillion of annual insurance premiums globally, $4 trillion alone in non-life premium, and global non-life premiums are growing annually due to economic expansion, emerging risks and exposures, increasing insurance demand and new coverages purchased by those that already buy insurance.

And added:

It's estimated there are tens of thousands of agencies and brokerage firms across our 5 major geographies, including 30,000 in the U.S. alone according to a leading consulting firm. Most of these firms have less than $25 million in annual revenue. Our family owned and have salespeople and colleagues with strong roots in their local communities.

For this reason, I think it's likely that growth will continue, organically and inorganically, as it has the past decade. During Q2 and the update calls, management guided for high, single-digit premium increases. With the global growth trends in specialty insurance that they noted, as well as improving margins as they scale, I think double-digit FCF growth is possible, particularly as they integrate AssuredPartners.

I'll value AJG with a Discounted Cash Flow calculation under the following assumptions then:

  • $2B as baseline FCF (trimming from $3B to factor some regular M&A)
  • 15% CAGR first 5 years
  • 10% CAGR the next 5 (less cost savings after Assured is integrated)
  • Terminal multiple of 15 (pricing for moderate growth in distant future)
  • Discount rate of 10% (typical return of a market index)

These are very healthy growth assumptions for a business as large as theirs, and I think the recent price near $310 at the $79B market cap can offer positive returns over time but perhaps not favorably in comparison to an index.

Their own data show how premium growth (which increases their commissions) isn't always positive. In periods of economic distress, it's negative. While they have avoided the deepest lows of the average, they face some cyclicality that can slow growth. As these acquisitions are normally financed with debt, I think it's fair to avoid being over-eager on a stock like this.

AJG isn't the only insurance broker growing through M&A, and they will face competition as other players consolidate. I think investors would get an entry price that braces for some of these headwinds. While they don't bear underwriting risk, paying too much create just as much risk to long-term returns.

That's I'm considering it fairly valued for now and waiting for a better discount.

Conclusion

AJG executes its M&A strategy well. It's easy to love insurance, especially the distributor side, but the trick is not to overpay for the growth ahead. While brokers are capital-light compared to insurance, that's not completely true if they're doing leveraged buyouts. Because I want more of a discount to ensure the returns are good and risk is smaller, I rate AJG a Hold for now.

data.title: Why Energy Prices Are Finally Set To Fall Next Year
data.description:
By Warren Patterson

Further OPEC+ supply sees oil surplus grow

Oil prices have been choppy over the past month, with Brent trading briefly above US$70/bbl on the back of geopolitical and sanction risks building in the market. But the move was short-lived, and the market came under renewed pressure following growing noise that OPEC+ will bring further supply onto the market more aggressively than anticipated.

However, with the market already set to move into a large surplus from the fourth quarter of this year and remain there throughout 2026, we do not believe that additional supply from OPEC+ is needed. This gives us growing confidence in our view that oil prices will move lower next year, and we're maintaining our forecast of Brent averaging US$57/bbl.

OPEC+’s recent actions indicate the group remains focused on reclaiming market share. With fiscal breakeven oil prices significantly above current levels, the sustainability of this strategy is likely to come under greater scrutiny in 2026.

There are clear upside risks to the oil market, with the most prominent being the ongoing threat of sanctions and secondary tariffs targeting Russia. Currently, only India faces secondary tariffs for its purchases of Russian oil. However, a more aggressive stance from President Trump could place broader Russian supply at risk. Another key upside risk also stems from Russia on the supply side, specifically the potential for disruptions caused by Ukrainian drone attacks on Russian energy infrastructure. While most of these attacks have focused on refineries, there have been instances targeting port infrastructure, which could directly impact crude oil exports.

Weak Asian LNG demand takes pressure off European gas prices

European gas prices have remained largely under pressure. TTF continues to trade in the low EUR 30s despite Europe moving closer towards the 25/26 heating season. A recovery in Norwegian gas flows following the end of scheduled maintenance would have helped ease any supply concerns. In addition, Asian LNG demand, particularly from China, remains weak, which has helped ensure adequate supply for Europe as we approach the heating season.

EU gas storage ended September at more than 83% full, and with storage targets for the EU now more flexible, we should not expect upward pressure from buying to ensure the 90% storage target is met by 1 November. Ultimately, the outlook for natural gas prices through the winter is going to largely depend, of course, on the weather.

We expect European natural gas prices to trend lower through 2026 and particularly through 2027. The start-up of significant LNG export capacity should ensure that the market becomes increasingly well supplied.

The ramp-up of new additional LNG export capacity should also make it possible for the EU to go ahead with its plan to phase out Russian gas, without putting significant upward pressure on prices.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BSV: Monthly Income Now, Price Optionality If Forwards Drift Lower
data.description:
Introduction

Vanguard offers its ETF: BSV Vanguard Short-Term Bond (BSV), for anyone looking for exposure in the short stretch of the yield curve. The index they follow is the Bloomberg U.S. 1–5 Year Government/Credit Float Adjusted Index, which is comprised primarily of U.S. Treasury and agency debt, plus high credit quality (investment grade) corporate bonds with maturities between 1 and 5 years. In this way, they offer diversified exposure in quality fixed income (Treasury/Agency + corporate IG) concentrated in the tranche where the sensitivity to rates is lower than in medium or long maturities.

ETF management is done from a passive approach (index sampling): not all index bonds are purchased instead, the Vanguard team selects a representative basket that replicates the key benchmark factors, allowing cost containment and rotation without sacrificing fidelity with the index, keeping a low tracking error. Official documents explicitly state that BSV seeks to follow the Bloomberg U.S. 1–5 Year Government/Credit Float Adjusted Index and that it is passively managed on a sample basis.

On the other hand, the vehicle distributes monthly dividends from the coupon flow and the 0.03% commission is extremely low compared to its counterparts, something especially relevant in fixed income, where each base point of cost weighs on the net return. Both characteristics are highlighted in the file and the prospectus: monthly dividend and expense ratio of 0.03%.

Today, the portfolio is mostly supported by Treasury/Agencies (the "almost risk-free" part that helps moderate price volatility) and the rest is made up of corporate sectors investment grade (financial, industrial, utilities, among others) that provide credit premium (spread). The eligible universe of the benchmark, and therefore of the ETF, is denominated in USD, at a fixed rate, investment grade and within the 1–5 years window; that is, the high yield and the maturities over 5 years are excluded.

The vehicle can be revalued/depreciated mainly by three ways: coupons, interest rate price, credit (spreads).

  • Coupons. As mentioned above, BSV collects and distributes monthly dividends that come from the interest on the bonds in the portfolio; in a stable or declining rate environment, the flow of coupons is often the main driver of the total return in the short term.

  • Prices by interest rates. The price of bonds is sensitive to movements in returns on the 1–5 year tranche. The average duration of the fund is currently 2.6 years so that a 100 bp movement in the short curve translates into an impact of approximately 2.6% on the value of the portfolio. This lower volatility in the price with respect to the longer term tranches, fits with those investors looking for profitability in the coupons without worrying much about the movement of the position.

  • Risk premium (spreads). When the market demands a lower risk premium, spreads between "risk-free" bonds and corporate GIs narrow, and the corporate component contributes at a price; if it widens, it subtracts.

Finally, the roll-down of the curve can also provide a small plus to the investor. Exposed the characteristics of the vehicle, in practice BIV is aimed at forming the defensive part of the portfolios that provides recurrent income, low commissions and relative stability versus the long part of the curve and equity income.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

IMPLIED FORWARD RATES - TREASURY CURVE

In the implied forwards chart, we can see how since the beginning of 2025, in the tranche where the fund is maintained, they have fallen sharply from 4.8% – 4.7% to 3.9% – 3.8% for the tranche of 2 to 5 years and from 4.5% – 4.6% to 3.6% – 3.5% for the tranche of 1 to 2 years. The steepness of the short tranche, which is larger than that of the long tranche, suggests that the rate cut will be larger in the short tranche than in the 2-5 year period. To this day, this downward effect of the forward has favored ETF price action: as the market incorporated lower future rates, the spot IRRs of the short tranche were falling and bond prices were rising.

Today, with forwards at 3.5& - 3.9%, a further revaluation of the portfolio by interest rates, will depend on whether the reality is more dovish than has already been discounted in the price, whether by faster cuts, deep cuts or both. Moreover, in this scenario the portfolio reinvestment will lead to a gradual decrease of future dividends distributed.

This shift in expectations is evident in the ETF itself: the price of BSV has gone from $77.21 to $78.72 so far this year (+1.95%). It is important to note that the price

Important: The price simply underestimates the return captured, because each dividend reduces the price on the ex-div date; that is, a part of what the "market has put in price" is not seen in the quote, but has been paid in cash via monthly coupons. Add the dividends to the total price (return), and the "rate-drop rally" picture is more generous than that nominal +1.95%.

In both images we show the table of dividends distributed by the ETF. Observing the payments, during 2025 have been reflected increases compared to the second half of 2024 $0.24 – $0.26 versus $0.20 – $0.23 respectively per participation.

With the data in hand, the total for the last 12 months amounts to $2.94 per share (TTM), which is equivalent to an approximate TTM return of 3.74% taking the current price of $78.72 as a reference. That said, if we only look at 2025, the fund has distributed $2.24 and on that same price implies a YTD yield of about 2.84%, which would be annualized at a yield of about 3.8%.This data, reinforces the thesis that after the rise in rates of 2023–2024 the roll of the portfolio captured a higher coupon, raising the monthly income for much of 2025.

Looking ahead, with forwards around 3.5–3.9% for the 1–5 years, it is reasonable to think that carry will hold but soften as the fund rotates to new issues with somewhat lower IRRs.

ICE BofA OAS HEATMAP 21-DAY-X-SCORE HEATMAP

Next, we will focus on the corporate "leg" of the ETF and for this we will analyze that our heatmap of OAS (spreads) tells us: warmer colors represent widening compared to the recent average, while cold shades mark narrowing.

For a vehicle like BSV, which combines government and IG credit, this graph helps us discern where the widening/contraction pressure comes from in corporate spreads: when there are widespread narrowings in A and BBB (equally for AAA and AA but the weight is much less representative in BSV) the corporate part adds via price, while warm colors reflect widening in the spreads. In short, the graph helps us visualize whether the credit portion is rowing in favor or exercising resistance.

The most recent data from the heatmap show a fairly stabilized credit in investment grade: AAA appears in the neutral range, while A/AA moves close to normal and BBB (the most sensitive part) has shown temperate tones during the month of August but without sustained tension and already stabilized again in the cold zone. Therefore, the biggest pressure is currently concentrated on the AAA side.

With pressure concentrated on AAA and BBB already in the cold zone after the August upturn, it is feasible to say that the credit factor is not a brake to consider today and we can consider that the current environment for BSV is favorable: the corporate tranche continues to carry with limited volatility and the current "headwind" AAA remains moderate and with a tendency to further narrow. That's why the expected return will continue to be marked by carry plus roll-down, with the factor types as catalyst: if forwards come down again and spread by credit stays stable and even AAA improves, we can still scratch something for the price part.

Conclusion

That said, we currently conclude that a part of the type rally is already integrated into the price. On the other hand, the credit side is currently stable and does not pose a price threat and continues to carry with moderate volatility. From here, the future return that BSV can give will come mainly from the carry part plus the roll-down with the possibility of still obtaining profits by price (forwards even lower + narrowing of spreads). If you already have BSV in your portfolio, our recommendation is to keep it and in the case of not having ETF shares, we would recommend going in if you are looking purely to carry and oriented to a short term and value alternatives.

data.title: Brinker International: Double-Digit Growth Restaurant Company Priced At The Discount
data.description:
Brinker International, Inc. (NYSE:EAT) is a casual dining restaurant company, whose primary brands are Chili's Grill & Bar and Maggiano's Little Italy. This company owns, franchises, and operates different restaurant brands around the globe, creating a welcoming atmosphere for guests and a delicious Italian-American experience.

The company has appreciated by more than 50% over the past 12 months, and has significantly outperformed the benchmark. This has been driven by significant top and bottom-line growth, indicating compelling and high-demand restaurant services. EAT is a consumer discretionary stock, which faces positive tailwinds from a dovish Fed and easing interest cycle.

Brinker International trades at a forward P/E of roughly 12, highlighting a roughly 32% discount compared to the sector median of about 18x. This makes it an interesting opportunity for long-term investors to diversify in an undervalued name that may appreciate in the stock price if the US consumer strengthens and interest rates continue to come down.

I rate Brinker International a Buy, with a $155 12-month price target. This suggests about 23% upside and broader market outperformance. I think that it is impressive that the company shows fundamental strength despite uncertainties in the economy, and if the macro outlook improves, consumer sentiment may shift, and EAT could become a beneficiary of the positive improvements.

FQ4 2025 Snapshot: Strong Top And Bottom Line Growth Supported By Significant Demand

On August 13, the company reported its FQ4 2025 results. This has been a strong, double-beat quarter, highlighting phenomenal execution and significant demand in its restaurant services.

The company generated about $1.46 billion in revenue, underscoring 21% growth on a year-over-year basis, and $19 million above the estimates. The bottom-line came in at $2.49 in diluted EPS, more than a 50% increase versus $1.61 in the same quarter last year, and $0.02 above analysts' expectations.

In their supplemental presentation for FQ4 2025, management highlighted that Chili's restaurants experienced a massive increase from $1.07 billion in sales to roughly $1.33 billion over the past 12 months, underscoring about a 24% increase. On the downside, Maggiano's faced a minor decline versus the same quarter last year, from $124 million in sales to $122 million. Regardless, this is about a 10x smaller restaurant brand, and its Chili's has experienced a surge in demand, supporting the bullish outlook for the company.

To underline, these results are phenomenal, despite macroeconomic uncertainties around the US consumer. The other retail companies have highlighted consumer weakness, such as Chipotle in its previous quarterly report, with a 4% decline in comparable sales, and Target with about a 2% decline in the same metric. On the upside, this indicates that with the strengthening consumer, Brinker International may achieve even more rapid growth.

Management provided guidance for FY2026. The company anticipates $5.65 billion at the midpoint in revenue, and $10.20 at the midpoint in diluted EPS. This indicates about 5% growth in the top-line over the next year, and about 15% bottom-line expansion.

I think that these estimates indicate strong and continued demand despite consumer headwinds. I believe the company appears to be well-positioned to achieve the estimates, and with a dovish Fed and interest rate reductions, consumer sentiment may shift, resulting in a more favorable environment for EAT's business.

The Company Appears To Be Undervalued — I Think, The Market May Reprice It Sooner, Rather Than Later

EAT trades at a forward P/E of 12.22, which appears to be undervalued compared to the sector median of 17.86. The chart below indicates that the current valuation represents an average earnings multiple for the company, supporting my thesis of the possible multiple expansion.

The forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 0.83 implies 2x undervaluation versus the sector median of 1.78, supporting the bullish stance for the company and the positive outlook. The forward EV/EBITDA at 8.65 indicates about a 20% discount to peers of 10.74. I think if the company continues top and bottom line growth, Brinker International may rebound from these levels and trade at a closer to industry-like valuations.

The forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales at 1.28 and 0.99, respectively, reveal fair pricing for the stock. It trades roughly in line compared to the industry's average of 1.32 and 0.99. This is interesting and may suggest that the market expects continued revenue expansion, and may have already partially priced it in. This slightly offsets the bullish outlook and indicates that if the company continues with a low single-digit expansion, the stock may not be rewarded with a higher premium, despite the growth. On the other hand, if results come in better-than-anticipated, it could significantly contribute to a multiple expansion.

The company appears to be a medium-leveraged company with total obligations of $1.69 billion. This partially clouds the bullish narrative, as it indicates business is sensitive to a high-interest environment. The company has $18.90 million in cash, but that is insignificant compared to its debt.

The restaurant company paid $10.90 million in interest expense last quarter, which makes it a substantial financial burden. However, I think it could act as a short-term tailwind for the stock, taking into account a dovish Fed stance. The market anticipates two additional cuts of 0.25% by the end of the year, which, if it materializes, may drive a sentiment change for risk-on assets. Furthermore, if interest rates decline further, the company may be able to refinance its obligations, and that could result in lower interest expense, supporting bottom-line growth.

EAT has achieved 21.95% revenue growth over the past 12 months, which implies more than 7x outperformance versus peers at 2.82%, and justifies that the stock may start trading at a higher multiple. The forward revenue growth rate of about 11%, 3.4x above the industry's average of 3.16%, suggests that the trend is likely to continue, and supports the higher-end guidance provided by management.

EBITDA grew by 72% on a year-over-year basis, underscoring 30x outperformance compared to the sector median of 2.25%. The bottom line increased by 144% over the past year, significant outperformance versus peers of almost flat 0.24% growth, highlighting strong fundamentals and excellent execution by management. I also think that the EAT stock price performance may mirror strong bottom-line growth, which could lead to double-digit expansion and broader market outperformance.

The company operates at a gross profit margin (TTM) of 18.25%. This is below-market, underscoring 2x underperformance versus peers of 38.82%. However, the net income margin (TTM) of 7.12% balances the bearish narrative and highlights a 64% advantage over peers of 4.33%. The company generated $679 million in cash from operations (TTM), 2.3x higher than the industry's average of $293 million, supporting a bullish stance.

Overall, the restaurant company is trading at undervalued levels, while growing top and bottom-line at a double-digit pace. Its primary Chili's restaurant brand experiences a significant demand, and with tailwinds tied to the US consumer, EAT appears to be well-positioned to benefit from improved sentiment. I think the company may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking exposure to a high-growth consumer discretionary name at an undervalued level.

Wall Street anticipates $10.35 in diluted EPS for FY2026, which comes in line with my expectations. This would signal 16.32% bottom-line expansion over the 12-month period, roughly 60% above the benchmark's recent 10% annual EPS growth rate, justifying multiple expansion.

If we apply a 15x earnings multiple, indicating about 20% higher than it currently is, to a FY2026 EPS estimate of $10.35, we come in at about a $155 price target. I think the company deserves an elevated multiple given its double-digit top and bottom line growth, and tailwinds tied to the dovish Fed.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Brinker International that may significantly impact the stock price in the short term.

I think that rising food, energy, utility, and labor costs may significantly impact its operational margins and have an impact on the stock price. This is a risk and concern the majority of restaurants face, and EAT can raise prices to compensate for rising inflation or elevated costs. However, there is a limit before customers may choose an alternative. This is an area to monitor for investors, as if inflation remains sticky, this could weigh on the stock price.

The restaurant companies are highly dependent on consumer sentiment and spending. So far, based on the recent quarterly reports, despite the macroeconomic uncertainty, the company has achieved significant top and bottom-line expansion. However, if the outlook gets clouded by worries, it may erode, which could lead to weaker-than-anticipated future growth.

The company appears to be medium-leveraged. If the Fed decides to stick with a higher interest rate environment for longer, this may result in significant interest expense further. This could also put pressure on the stock price, as it moves along other interest-sensitive names.

If none of the above materializes, EAT stock may mirror bottom-line performance. I think the company is well-positioned for continued business improvement, and if the economic outlook strengthens, it may deliver stronger than guided results.

Conclusion: Growth Through Uncertainties Makes It A Compelling Opportunity

I think that Brinker International may appear to be an interesting opportunity for long-term investors who seek exposure to the restaurant business at an appealing valuation. The company's results are strong, despite the current US consumer weakness, which is expected to continue. I think the business is well-positioned to grow its top and bottom line further, and the stock price may mirror that performance.

EAT has tailwinds tied to a dovish Fed, which could sustain a positive sentiment for interest-sensitive names. It is a medium-leveraged company that slightly clouds the bullish narrative, but management has done an excellent job by paying down its long-term debt, so far, which is nearly 3x lower than in years prior. I think the trend is likely to continue, which supports the bullish outlook.

I rate Brinker International a Buy, with a $155 price target over the next 12 months. This implies roughly 23% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. 19 Wall Street analysts forecast a $178 price target over the next 12 months, highlighting a 40% upside. I favor a more conservative approach, but that target may also be a possibility.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 240: $24,000 Allocated, $2,636.52 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This was an interesting week in the market, as both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq climbed higher despite a backdrop of a U.S. government shutdown that commenced mid-week. The shutdown led to the postponement of the highly anticipated monthly jobs report, which was supposed to be released on Friday. There is speculation that the absence of critical data points will create an incomplete picture for the Fed, which may impact their cutting cycle. Depending on how long the shutdown lasts, there could be a significant economic impact on the economy, as GDP growth could slow and billions of dollars are lost each week. My ultimate concern is that roughly 800,000 to 900,000 federal employees have been furloughed and another 700,000 essential employees are being required to continue working without their regular paychecks. Every day that the shutdown lasts, there is an increased chance that cuts to the federal workforce will occur. Regardless of what anyone's personal feelings are about the size of government, the loss of jobs is negative for the economy, especially in a time when A.I. and robotics are on the rise. We have already seen unemployment increase to 4.3%, and if the shutdown and productivity from technology cause unemployment to increase, it would increase the chances of a recession. I believe that a deal will be reached over the next 2 weeks, but I am paying close attention to the labor market. Ultimately, I don’t believe the stock market will be impacted in the short term, and I feel that Q3 earnings will drive the direction of the market rather than what is happening in Washington, D.C. No matter what happens, I believe that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is positioned to do well, and I am excited to see how the remainder of the year progresses.

The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq snapped their losing streaks this week despite a government shutdown. After allocating $24,000 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 240 at $31,943.52. The account's profitability increased by 3.39% ($260.29) to $7,943.46, which is a return on invested capital of 33.10%. This week was reader suggestion week, and I added the NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $116.32 of dividend income in week 240. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $21.31 (0.81%) to $2,636.52. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $5,103.98 of income since its inception, which is 21.27% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. This week helped offset the losses from the previous 2 weeks, and I am closing in on some additional milestones. Regardless of what happens with the government shutdown, I believe that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is positioned well for the future, and by reinvesting the recurring income, I will benefit from the powers of compounding. It’s going to be interesting to see how the remainder of the year plays out, but I think that there could be close to $2,750 of forward dividend income being generated by the time 2025 comes to an end.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio snapped its 2-week losing streak this week as it added $260.29 of profitability. The account is up $7,943.46 (33.10%) and has an account value of $31,943.46 while generating $2,636.53 of forward dividend income. Currently the account has a portfolio yield of 8.25% and a 10.99% yield on cost. I believe that these results are very strong from starting the account with $100 with the premise of investing in income-producing assets rather than focusing on capital appreciation. By allocating capital each week, I have been able to build a robust income-producing portfolio on a budget by focusing on income and risk mitigation. I am excited to see how the next several years unfold, as there will be a tremendous amount of capital being reinvested back into the portfolio along with the weekly allocations.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $951.03 (36.07%)

  • Equities $590.41 (22.39%)

  • CEFs $420.37 (15.94%)

  • REITs $417.13 (15.82%)

  • BDCs $257.59 (9.77%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 40 weeks in 2025, $1,785.63 of dividend income was produced, which is 110.01% of the total income from 2024. There were 567 positions that paid a dividend, which is 85.75% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $116.32 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $44.64. This is an increase of $13.43 (43.01%) YoY, and there have been 3 weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I’m really excited for the rest of September to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

There was a whirlwind of income generated in the last 2 trading days of September, which has become the largest month of income in the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio’s history. I ended up producing $209.90 of dividend income this month, which I believe will set the stage for Q4 being the largest quarter of income. The days of generating less than $200 of dividend income per month are likely in the past, and a new standard will be set. It’s going to be interesting to see how Q4 unfolds, as there were 2 months in Q3 that exceeded $200 of dividend income. As I continue to allocate more capital, I think the monthly norm will become $225 or $250 much sooner than I had expected.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 44 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There is $504.79 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I am making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding to REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It’s difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, as I believe that technology is going to heat up throughout earnings season and that there are still a lot of deals in the REIT segment. Individual equities now represent 39.17% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.39% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.83% of the portfolio and generate 77.61% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

I am quite happy that the top 20 positions are rounding out a bit further. While all my positions are under the 5% threshold, I really want to get Altria Group (MO) to represent less of the portfolio, considering I haven’t added new shares to the position in over a year and a half. One of the keys to this portfolio’s success has been the level of diversification. Eventually, the top 3 positions will get smaller, and I will feel more comfortable allocating capital toward them. I am expecting the top 20 positions to change a bit next year, and I am hoping that I can get all the positions a bit more even in the future. While I have allowed myself a 5% maximum, I really want to have a well-rounded portfolio that can mitigate risk well.

This week the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) and AT&T (T) were knocked out of the top 10 by Pfizer (PFE) and the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI). There were also several dividends paid, which changed the dynamics of the top 10 holdings this week. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,837.91, and they finished the week with a value of $9,257.86. This is an ROI of $2,419.95 (35.39%). There has been $1,280.27 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 18.72% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $695.48 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10.17%. These positions represent 28.98% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 26.38% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 240 Additions

This week was reader suggestion week, and several people have told me to look into the other ETFs that NEOS Investments has created. I looked at the NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF and decided to add it to the portfolio.

  • I believe that right now is a good time to add exposure to REITs, as a lower rate environment will lower their cost of capital and make their dividend income look more attractive.

  • IYRI utilizes a data-driven call option strategy to generate its 11.17% distribution rate from its portfolio of REITs.

  • I felt that the portfolio was strong, and there were several REITs I wanted to invest in, so I felt this was a good option to gain broad-based exposure.

  • I think that IYRI will lead to both capital appreciation and double-digit yields.

Week 241 gameplan

Next week I will probably add the Adams Diversified Equity Fund to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

Conclusion

September ended up being the largest month of dividend income to date, and the chart below is looking very bullish as the S-curve of income is getting steeper. I think that there is a chance that I will generate between $650 and $700 of income in Q4 and set the stage for producing more than $3,000 of income in 2026. All of this was accomplished starting with $100 and adding $100 per week to income-producing assets. Anyone can build an income-producing portfolio; all it takes is time and persistence. I think there are a lot of opportunities in the market and that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is positioned well for the future. I am looking forward to earnings season and seeing what the Fed does at the next FOMC meeting. I have a feeling that both will be very bullish for the market. Please leave all your comments and suggestions in the comment section below as I try to interact with everyone throughout the week.

data.title: Kraft Heinz: Uninspiring And Lacking In Conviction
data.description:
Overview

The Kraft Heinz Company (NASDAQ:KHC) was originally formed from the 2015 merger of Kraft Foods Group and H.J. Heinz. It was backed by Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway and 3G Capital, combining two iconic portfolios of packaged foods under one roof. Yet, ten years later, the company has underperformed nearly every major peer, losing roughly seventy percent of its value from its post-merger peak and now trading near all-time lows. Recently, the management team announced that the board approved a split into two independent companies via spin off, a move that has drawn confusion from pundits and shareholders.

Kraft Heinz owns a long list of recognizable brands including Kraft, Heinz, Oscar Mayer, Philadelphia, Lunchables, Velveeta, Maxwell House, Kool-Aid, and Jell-O. The business remains profitable and continues to pay a sizable dividend, but growth has been absent for years. As a result, the stock has become a case study in what happens when brand nostalgia fails to translate into modern relevance.

Investment Thesis

Kraft Heinz sits in an awkward middle ground. It is neither a growth story nor a clear turnaround story. The company indeed generates strong cash flow and maintains a high dividend yield that is well-covered, but it operates in categories that have lost consumer momentum. The 2015 merger has done little to create shareholder value, and sales and profits have been effectively stagnant for the decade since the move.

The current leadership has announced a plan to split Kraft Heinz into two companies: one company will focus on sauces, spreads, seasoning, and shelf-stable meals, and the other will focus on grocery staples. Warren Buffett, still the largest shareholder through Berkshire Hathaway, has publicly voiced disappointment with the decision, questioning whether separation solves anything. He is right to be skeptical. Spinoffs can unlock value when distinct growth trajectories exist or the different businesses are drastically different from each other, but Kraft Heinz’s problem is not purely structural, it is strategic. The brands are arguably losing relevance, and a corporate breakup does nothing to change that.

At the same time, Kraft Heinz is not in distress. The company remains profitable, cash generative, and trades at an undeniably cheap multiple. Management has managed the finances of the company responsibly and cautiously over the last ten years. The question is not whether it will survive, but whether the business has any path to meaningful reinvention in a world that increasingly favors fresh, healthy, and premium food offerings. At the current valuation, there is good value for the underlying business, but the plan to split calls into question the true path forward for truly creating shareholder value.

Financial Performance

Revenue has stagnated since the merger. In 2016, Kraft Heinz generated roughly $26.3 billion in revenue, compared to $25.3 billion over the last twelve months. Gross margins have zig-zagged lower in that time, which I believe is an indicator of the competitive positioning of their brands. Operating income has declined more sharply, further underscoring the lack of operating leverage.

The balance sheet appears stable but uninspiring. The company holds approximately $1.6 billion in cash and $21 billion in total debt. Current assets comfortably cover current liabilities, and retained earnings have gradually improved over the last few years, suggesting competent management of cash flow. However, more than half of the company’s total assets are intangible. While this isn't a bad thing per-se, it definitely is not a positive in my book and is worth taking into account when evaluating a company.

From a profitability standpoint, Kraft Heinz is not struggling to meet its obligations. The business is steady and cash-rich, but flat. The absence of growth is the defining feature of its financial profile. With all of this being considered, I find the fact that the dividend has remained flat since 2019 to be appropriate. If the dividend had been being raised this whole time, I would have much more critical concerns about the capital allocation priorities of the business. Instead, debt has been paid down over the last decade, and management has done a good job of keeping financial house in order. On the flip side, this has also manifested itself in a lack of splashy acquisitions and notable brand innovation. KHC is truly just plodding along, which leaves me largely uninspired. I do like how they have seemed to avoid the empire-building trap, but at least empire-building creates a feeling of "building", rather than maintaining.

Valuation

By most traditional metrics, Kraft Heinz looks cheap. The stock trades below 10x earnings and offers a dividend yield above 6%, which is the highest it has traded at since March 2020. Price-to-book sits below one, and nearly every multiple is at or below the company’s five-year average. Seeking Alpha’s quant system assigns it an “A” for valuation, which is understandable given the numbers.

However, value without a catalyst can be a trap. The dividend has not been raised since being cut in 2019, and there is little reason to expect an increase or cut soon. The payout ratio appears healthy, but the lack of organic expansion leaves little to imagine for future compounding with the existing brand portfolio. At current levels, Kraft Heinz is not overvalued, it is simply priced for stagnation. For investors who want to clip a coupon a few hundred bps above the 10y with equity like volatility and potential for future upside, I see how this one can make sense here. However, I am a bit discouraged by the lack of clarity from management on how the portfolio will be more meaningfully positioned to take advantage of changing consumer interests.

Berkshire's ownership provides a psychological floor, but it does not create upside. Investors buying Kraft Heinz today are buying a bond substitute with brand risk, not a business in transition.

Risks

The risks here are straightforward. The first is slowly materializing structural irrelevance, wherein the stock (or the two remaining tickers post-split) continue to stay stagnant. When the split occurs, perhaps we will see that one of the companies has a superior portfolio with robust margins, which I could see outperforming the other ticker. However, in the absence of this information, we are left to wonder. Many of Kraft Heinz’s brands feel dated, and consumer preferences continue to shift toward fresh, premium, and health-oriented options. The second is strategic drift. Management’s decision to pursue a breakup suggests a lack of confidence in the existing structure, despite the rose-colored glasses on display in the most recent earnings presentation.

The third risk is macro perception. The rise of GLP-1 drugs has introduced new anxiety across the packaged food sector. Whether those concerns prove justified or not, sentiment toward highly processed foods is weakening for both investors and end consumers. Finally, while the dividend appears safe for now, the company’s high debt load limits flexibility if profitability declines further or if large acquisitions are chased with additional debt.

None of these risks are catastrophic on their own, but together they form a picture of a business fighting against long-term headwinds rather than harnessing them.

Technical Analysis

Kraft Heinz’s chart tells a story of slow decay, although it has been largely devoid of big volatility the last few years. The stock peaked shortly after the merger, collapsed into the 2019 dividend cut, rebounded briefly after the pandemic, and has since trended back toward its prior lows.

Recent price action shows the shares trading within a newly formed downtrend with 4 bounces off both resistance and support. While the long term chart makes it appear that the current price is at a potential long-term bottom, the nearer-term picture looks much more bleak. From a near-term technical standpoint, KHC remains a falling knife with no clear breakout structure. The current setup offers no reason to expect a trend reversal in the near term.

Conclusion: Avoid

Kraft Heinz is not a broken business, it is a business seemingly without clear direction and clear conviction in the future. The brands still carry recognition, the dividend still pays, and the balance sheet remains intact. But without growth, meaningful relevance, or innovation that inspires, the company’s value proposition is limited.

At this valuation, huge downside might be contained, but the upside is also capped, especially without clarity about the different financial properties of the split. The proposed breakup may provide a short-term headline, but it does not solve the core issue: the management team might have played it too safe the last few years, and the split now reveals a lack of conviction. Until Kraft Heinz demonstrates an emphasis on the underlying brands, not just finances, the stock is unlikely to reward long-term investors.

For now, Kraft Heinz sits firmly in avoid territory.

data.title: Apple's Implicitly Expected EPS Growth: Realistic Or Bubble?
data.description:
Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) trailing PE ratio of approximately 38.80 implicitly reflects very optimistic expectations for future earnings growth. What precisely are these expectations? Are these expectations likely to be realized?

To address these questions, our first step will be to "reverse engineer" the EPS growth expectations that are currently embedded in Apple's stock price. Our second step will be to assess whether and to what extent expectations of abnormal EPS growth are justified. Our third step will be to perform a scenario analysis and to compare these scenarios to Apple's historical EPS performance and consensus EPS estimates. Finally, we will assess Apple's reward-to-risk profile.

Implied EPS Growth: The Analytical Framework

Our proprietary Abnormal Incremental Returns & Growth [AIRG] methodology provides an ideal framework for "reverse engineering" the real EPS growth rate expectation that's implicit in Apple's current share price. Our analysis requires three basic assumptions:

  1. A real cost of equity.
  2. An explicitly defined forecast period for abnormal EPS.
  3. In the “continuing period” beyond the explicit forecast horizon, EPS growth is "normal."

We'll now examine each of these assumptions independently.

1. Real Cost of Equity (RCOE): 6.50%

In estimating the intrinsic PE ratio of any stock, it's best to use the real (inflation-adjusted) cost of equity as the required real rate of return. We estimate Apple's real (inflation-adjusted) cost of equity (RCOE) as the sum of three components:

  • Real risk-free rate: 2.50%. We use the real (inflation-adjusted) 30-year TIPS yield as our benchmark for the real risk-free rate.
  • Equity market premium: 4.00%. The long-term historical equity premium relative to 30-year Treasury notes is approximately 4.50%. However, 4.00% is more consistent with estimates of the implied equity premium in recent decades and with independent estimates of the prospective equity premium.
  • Idiosyncratic premium/discount: 0.00%. Based on a combination of corporate bond spreads, implied volatility, and measures of fundamental risk (e.g. financial and operating leverage), we believe the current required rate of return required by marginal investors in Apple is roughly equal to that of the overall market, despite Apple’s slightly higher price volatility.

Please note that although our base-case estimate of Apple's RCOE is 6.50%, our analysis below will consider the sensitivity of the estimate of price-implied real EPS growth to the estimated value of the RCOE.

2. Abnormal Growth Period: 10 Years

We have set our explicit abnormal earnings growth forecast period for Apple to 10 years, reflecting two considerations.

  • Proven moat: Apple has historically demonstrated an extraordinary ability to sustain its competitive advantages (ecosystem lock-in and brand loyalty) over time.
  • Maximum forecast period: We believe that 10 years represents the outer limit for a reasonable forecast of abnormal earnings growth in the rapidly evolving consumer technology industry.

3. Assumptions Beyond Year 10

  • Steady state. Beyond the 10th year, it's assumed that real EPS grows at a "normal" rate.
  • A normal real EPS growth rate is equal to the earnings retention rate multiplied by the real cost of equity.
  • Normal Terminal PE. The intrinsic forward PE ratio of a stock growing at a normal rate is the reciprocal of the stock's real COE. In this case, the intrinsic PE of a company with a real COE of 6.50% is 15.38. This is therefore the terminal forward PE multiple for Apple applied at the end of year 10.

Apple's Implied EPS Growth: 13.89%

Based on the parameters above, the expected 10-year real EPS growth rate that's implicit in Apple's current stock price is 13.89% per annum. This assumes a continuation of its current retention rate of 84.53% (Payout ratio of 15.47%).

The following is an analysis of the sensitivity of the estimate of the implied real EPS growth rate to the RCOE, plus a decomposition (normal vs. abnormal growth):

Sensitivity Table: Implied 10-Year Real EPS Growth Decomposition

Apple's price-implied expected 10-year real EPS growth rate of 13.89% (abnormal 8.40%) implies an expectation that the company will be able to sustain exceptionally high returns (above COE) on incremental retained earnings for a full decade.

Context: History and Forecasts

In evaluating the reasonableness of Apple's implied EPS growth rate, it's instructive to compare it against its own historical performance as well as forward-looking analysts' forecasts.

  • Historical growth: Over the past 10 years (FY2015-2025E), Apple achieved a strong nominal EPS CAGR of roughly 14.45%. The geometric average annual CPI inflation rate over this period was approximately 3.13%. This translates to approximately 10.97% in real terms.
  • Median forecast: The median sell-side analyst forecast for the next five years projects nominal EPS growth of approximately 10.5%. The current 5-year TIPS breakeven inflation rate is 2.42%. This translates to 7.89% in real terms.

Crucially, the price-implied 10-year real EPS growth rate (13.89%) is significantly higher than both its historical 10-year real growth (10.97%) and the consensus five-year forecast (7.89%). This suggests the market is pricing in a significant acceleration of growth and a major “upside surprise growth surprise” relative to the median sell-side forecast.

Analysis of Potential Drivers of Abnormal Incremental Returns and Abnormal Growth

Apple's stock price clearly reflects expectations of protracted abnormal incremental returns and abnormal EPS growth. Such performance can only be achieved if a company possesses an extraordinary moat built from sustainable competitive advantages. Is there a solid fundamental basis for these expectations?

Apple does, in fact, enjoy one of the strongest consumer technology moats in the world.

Apple's "Walled Garden" Ecosystem:

  • Brand loyalty and aspirational status: Apple has cultivated a premium brand identity associated with quality, design, and status. This allows the company to command premium pricing and maintain exceptional margins despite operating in highly competitive hardware markets.
  • High switching costs: The core of the moat is the iOS operating system. The friction involved in leaving the Apple ecosystem (e.g., iMessage, iCloud, App Library synchronization) creates high switching costs, leading to very high customer retention rates.
  • Vertical integration: Apple's tight control over the entire user experience — from custom silicon (A-series and M-series chips) to the operating system and services — results in a seamless user experience and provides significant performance and efficiency advantages.
  • Services growth: The massive and affluent installed base of Apple devices provides a platform for delivering high-margin services revenue (App Store, Apple Music, iCloud, Advertising, etc.). These services enhance the stickiness of the entire ecosystem. Most importantly, high margin revenue from services improves the company’s overall profitability profile.

There is no question that Apple currently possesses a powerful moat. The real question is: Can Apple leverage this moat to accelerate growth in a mature smartphone market? Could a combination of regulation and competition penetrate Apple’s “walled garden” and eventually drive its profitability to more normal levels?

The Bull Case: The AI Super-Cycle

In a bull-case scenario, the introduction of "Apple Intelligence" reinvigorates the hardware business, driving a significant upgrade cycle, while the Services segment benefits from the growth of AI-based services.

We project a 16% real CAGR over a 10-year period as a bull-case scenario. This growth rate is significantly above the consensus five-year forecast (7.89% real) and the historical real 10-year growth rate (10.97%).

Bull-Case Drivers:

  • The "AI-on-device" super cycle: In this scenario, Apple integrates compelling Generative AI features deeply into iOS and its hardware (leveraging its silicon advantage for on-device processing). Apple develops "must-have" functionality that drives a massive iPhone upgrade cycle, shortening the replacement timeline.
  • Services acceleration and pricing power: Growth in the high-margin services division accelerates, driven by the introduction of new AI-enhanced service offerings.
  • Emerging markets execution: To achieve the bull-case scenario, Apple would have to successfully increase its market share gains in high-growth regions such as India and elsewhere in Asia.
  • Margin expansion: Continued consumer preference for high-end "Pro" model iPhones and the continued growth of high-margin services revenue lead to significant expansion of overall operating margins.

Bull Case Valuation:

If we assume Apple achieves 16% real annual EPS growth for the next 10 years, the intrinsic forward PE ratio derived from our model is 34.90x. Compared to the current forward P/E of 29.85x, this implies a bull case upside of +16.9%.

The Moderate Bear Case: Stagnation and Regulation

Given the valuation of its shares, Apple's investors could earn disappointing returns in a positive fundamental scenario that is simply "strong," rather than "exceptional." A moderate "bear-case" scenario would involve a failure to reinvigorate hardware sales and regulatory actions impairing the Services moat.

A robust 9% real CAGR over 10 years could be achievable in such a moderately strong scenario. This growth rate is slightly below the historical growth (10.97% real), slightly above the consensus forecast (7.89% real), but significantly below the price-implied expected EPS growth rate of 13.89%.

Bear Case Drivers:

  • Hardware stagnation and lengthening cycles: In this scenario, new features, including "Apple Intelligence," won’t be revolutionary enough to drive a significant upgrade cycle. Smartphone replacement cycles continue to lengthen, leading to stagnant iPhone unit sales.
  • Regulatory headwinds impair services moat: Global regulatory actions (e.g., the Digital Markets Act in the EU, DOJ actions in the US) force changes to the App Store model (e.g., lower commission rates, side-loading). This significantly impairs the growth and profitability of the high-margin services segment.
  • China risk (competition and geopolitics): Apple faces intense competition from local high-end smartphone manufacturers in China (e.g., Huawei). Geopolitical tensions further erode Apple's market share in this critical region.
  • AI perceived as lagging: Apple's AI offerings are perceived by consumers as lagging behind competitors (Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT)/OpenAI (OPENAI)), diminishing the appeal of new hardware and services.

Bear Case Valuation:

In the scenario outlined above a 9% real EPS growth rate over 10 years would be a fantastic outcome. However, in this scenario, the intrinsic forward PE drops to 21.74x from the current forward P/E of 30.80. This implies a bear case downside of -31.0%.

Overall Assessment of Reward-to-Risk Profile

Our scenario analysis above does not represent an independent assessment of different potential outcomes. Rather, it represents a projection of the sorts of scenarios that would be required to achieve the posited real EPS growth rates. This scenario analysis presents an unfavorable risk/reward profile: +16.9% upside vs. -31.0% downside—a reward-to-risk ratio of 0.55 to 1.00.

In assessing the probabilities associated with this range of possible outcomes, we think that they're significantly skewed to the downside. We think it's more likely that EPS growth will be below the implied growth rate. Indeed, we think that merely matching the median estimate of 7.79% real EPS growth would be a major positive accomplishment. Such a scenario would imply an intrinsic PE of 18.52 and a downside to the current price of roughly 40%.

Therefore, we do not think Apple's stock is attractive on an "expected return" basis.

Furthermore, based on our own application of the "margin of safety" principle, we seek a reward-to-risk ratio of at least 2:1 and an upside-downside absolute differential of at least 25%. Apple's stock fails dramatically on both of these criteria.

Having said that, we do not think that the demand structure underlying Apple's share price is inherently unstable (i.e., susceptible to a crash). As long as an "AI Super-Cycle" narrative remains plausible, Apple’s current valuation is likely to continue to be supported by an "optimistic fringe" of market participants.

Conclusion

An investment in Apple at today's prices is ultimately a bet that Apple can achieve abnormal returns on incremental investments, which will, in turn, enable it to deliver abnormally high real EPS growth of 13.89% for the next 10 years. This growth rate implies a major acceleration of growth and an upside surprise relative to sell-side estimates that are notorious for being too optimistic.

On balance, we think it's quite unlikely that Apple can deliver on the real EPS expectations implied by its current price. While the growth expectation is at least minimally plausible, the risk/reward profile is unfavorable.

All factors considered, we rate Apple as a "Sell" for managers who are index-sensitive. We rated Apple’s shares as a “Sell” on March 28, and the stock has underperformed the S&P 500 by 2.75% since that time. We project continued underperformance going forward. On a relative basis, we believe that the stock is less attractive than Alphabet, Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), Microsoft, or Nvidia (NVDA), which we have written about recently. For investors who seek superior risk-adjusted returns, as well as attractive absolute long-term returns, we do not think that Apple's shares offer a sufficiently compelling value proposition.

At Successful Portfolio Strategy, we're concentrated on finding opportunities that offer better reward-to-risk profiles within the US market, and particularly outside the United States.

data.title: Celestica: A Likely Q3 Beat May Not Spark Another Rally
data.description:
I'd Not Buy Celestica Before Its Upcoming Q3 Release

I know that many bulls associate strong earnings beats with subsequent rallies, and in most cases, it does happen this way: a company shows a double-beat, and then its stock price updates to a new ATH in a matter of days.

But it's not always working like that.

Celestica's (NYSE:CLS) Q4 2024, for example, was a double-beating quarter, just like all other quarters since late FY2021. But it failed to be in line with the magnitude of the previous quarters - the EPS and revenue beats were only 0.48% and 5.13%, respectively, which was below the average of the past beats, and so the market punished the stock price quite meaningfully before CLS reported for its Q1 2025 and proved that its business momentum isn't going anywhere.

I know that the above price action of early 2025 was superimposed on the broader market's weakness, which only exacerbated the downturn, but still, Celestica's meltdown of over 44% followed its great quarterly results, and there were limited signs of fundamental weakness that could have explained that move.
